Qlankr — Frameworks

What We Learned
Building an AI QA Tool

Magic automations, disappointing gotchas, and one decision we're glad we made — four frameworks, four stories.

① LangGraph ② GitNexus ③ FastAPI ④ Custom SVG Graph
The Project

Qlankr in 30 seconds

AI QA tool for game studios. Give it a GitHub repo + a PR URL → it analyzes which components are affected, what risks exist, and which tests to run.

① LangGraph
Powers the AI agent loop — Claude calls tools, observes results, repeats until done
② GitNexus
Indexes the repo into a local call graph — the agent queries it to understand blast radius
③ FastAPI
Streams agent steps and indexing progress to the browser in real time via SSE
④ Custom SVG Graph
Planned to use Sigma.js for graph visualization — ended up writing our own renderer
01
Framework 1 — LangGraph

What is LangGraph?

LangGraph is a framework for building stateful AI agent loops. You give it a model and a list of tools — it handles the loop: the model decides what tool to call, LangGraph calls it, passes the result back, and repeats until the model stops.

In Qlankr
The agent receives a PR URL, has access to GitHub tools (read the diff) and GitNexus tools (query the call graph), and keeps calling tools until it has enough context to submit a QA impact report.
Why not a plain loop?
LangGraph handles tool schema serialization, message history, streaming events, and the ReAct reasoning format — everything that would be boilerplate in a hand-rolled loop.
01
Framework 1 — LangGraph · Magic

Two lines give you a full agent loop

create_react_agent wires the model, tools, and system prompt together. astream_events emits a typed event for every step — we filter for on_tool_start to stream live tool calls to the frontend as the agent works.

# The entire agent setup
agent = create_react_agent(model=llm, tools=all_tools, prompt=SystemMessage(SYSTEM_PROMPT))

# Stream tool calls to the frontend in real time
async for event in agent.astream_events({"messages": [HumanMessage(pr_url)]}, version="v2"):
    if event["event"] != "on_tool_start":
        continue
    yield AgentStepEvent(tool=event["name"], summary=_tool_summary(event["name"], event["data"]["input"]))
No glue code for the tool loop. No manual JSON parsing. No retry logic. The frontend shows "Reading PR #42 → Checking blast radius: PlayerController → …" as it happens.
01
Framework 1 — LangGraph · Disappointment

No graceful exit from the event stream

When the agent calls submit_analysis (its final action) we break out of astream_events. That cancels LangGraph's in-flight background task for that tool call — producing a CancelledError traceback in the logs on every single run, even when everything worked.

  • The result is already delivered — the error is noise, not a real failure
  • Tried catching asyncio.CancelledError — that swallowed real cancellations and broke the agent
  • There's no "drain gracefully and stop" API on the event stream — break is the only exit
LangGraph gives you great tooling for starting an agent — but no clean way to stop mid-stream without side effects. We accepted it as background noise.
Framework 2 — GitNexus

What is GitNexus?

A local code intelligence engine. One command indexes any repo into a knowledge graph — every file, function, import, call chain, and execution flow. Fully local. No API key. No code leaves the machine.

  • Parsing — AST via Tree-sitter (C++, C#, Python, Go, JS/TS, Lua, Rust…)
  • Resolution — cross-file import and call chain linking
  • Clustering — Leiden algorithm groups semantically related files
  • Processes — entry point → full call graph tracing
  • Search — BM25 + semantic embeddings, all local
gitnexus analyze ./repo — the agent queries it immediately via MCP tools.
02
Framework 2 — GitNexus · Cool thing

The agent never reads every file

Five MCP tools let the agent query the graph instead of reading files manually. impact alone replaces what would take dozens of file reads.

  • impact(symbol) — blast-radius: risk level (LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH / CRITICAL), affected processes, depth breakdown. Symbol name only — not a file path.
  • context(symbol) — every function that calls this one, and every function it calls (360° view)
  • query(query) — BM25 + semantic search over execution flows; returns ranked processes and symbol context
  • cypher(query) — raw Cypher against KuzuDB for anything the above tools don't cover
  • detect_changes — maps changed files to affected symbols. Local git diff only — useless for remote PRs.
impact tells the agent where to look. context tells it what connects. cypher fills the gaps. A PR analysis that would need 30+ file reads takes 5–8 tool calls.
02
Framework 2 — GitNexus · Disappointments

Two things that hurt in production

1. Tree-sitter is a C++ library compiled from source at install time.

GitNexus uses Tree-sitter under the hood. On a fresh Docker image the npm install fails — no C++ build tools. We had to add build-essential and python3-dev to the Dockerfile just to install an npm package.

# Dockerfile — before we figured this out, npm install just crashed
RUN apt-get install -y build-essential python3-dev
RUN npm install -g gitnexus   # now compiles Tree-sitter successfully

2. Each PR analysis costs ~$1.

The agent uses Claude Sonnet and makes 10–25 tool calls per PR, each with a large context window. At scale this adds up fast — not suitable for high-volume usage without caching or a cheaper model for lighter PRs.

03
Framework 3 — FastAPI

What is FastAPI?

Python web framework built on Starlette and Pydantic. We use it as the backend API — but the interesting part isn't the REST endpoints. It's streaming long-running operations to the browser in real time.

The problem
Repo indexing takes minutes. PR analysis streams tool calls one by one. You can't return a single HTTP response — the user needs to see progress as it happens.
The solution
FastAPI's StreamingResponse wraps any async generator. We yield SSE-formatted strings directly — no websocket setup, no message queue, no pub/sub.
03
Framework 3 — FastAPI · Magic

Async generators as SSE streams

The agent yields Pydantic events. FastAPI serializes and streams them. The entire pipeline — agent tool call → Pydantic model → SSE string → browser — is wired with almost no glue code.

async def stream_response(gen):
    async for event in gen:
        event_type = getattr(event, "type", "message")
        yield f"event: {event_type}\ndata: {event.model_dump_json()}\n\n"

@app.post("/analyze")
async def analyze(request):
    return StreamingResponse(
        stream_response(run_agent(request.pr_url)),
        media_type="text/event-stream",
    )
Same pattern works for both endpoints: /index streams indexing stages, /analyze streams agent steps. One helper function handles both.
03
Framework 3 — FastAPI · Gotcha

The browser's SSE client only supports GET

The browser's native EventSource API only works with GET requests. Our endpoints need POST to send a body. So we can't use the standard SSE client at all — we had to hand-roll SSE parsing over a fetch() stream.

❌ Doesn't work
// EventSource = GET only
const es = new EventSource("/analyze");
// Can't send { pr_url: "..." } 🚫
✓ What we built
const resp = await fetch("/analyze", {
  method: "POST", body: JSON.stringify(req)
});
const reader = resp.body.getReader();
// manually parse "\n\n" delimited chunks
FastAPI's streaming works perfectly — this is a browser API constraint. Worth knowing before designing any SSE endpoint that needs a request body.
04
Framework 4 — Graph Visualization

What is it?

The knowledge graph visualization shows the indexed repo as an interactive graph — file nodes grouped by community cluster, edges representing import relationships. The user can pan, zoom, and click clusters to explore the codebase structure before running a PR analysis.

Planned stack
Sigma.js — WebGL/Canvas graph renderer
Graphology — graph data structure
ForceAtlas2 — force-directed layout algorithm running in a web worker
What we actually built
A fully custom SVG renderer — zero Sigma imports. Deterministic layout: clusters spread horizontally, file nodes arranged vertically below each cluster. Bézier curve edges, viewBox-based pan/zoom, ~200 lines total.
The data comes from KuzuDB — a local embedded graph database GitNexus writes to during indexing. No cloud, no external API, the data never leaves the machine. To get nodes and edges into the visualization, we query KuzuDB through GitNexus's MCP tools — there's no shortcut, even GitNexus's own UI does the same.
04
Framework 4 — Graph Visualization · Magic

60fps pan/zoom without re-rendering the graph

Zoom and pan update the SVG viewBox on every mouse event — too frequent for React state (re-renders on every pixel moved, visibly laggy on large graphs). But React still needs to know the final position for other components.

Solution: ref for immediate imperative DOM writes during mouse events, state updated only on mouse release — one re-render per drag, not hundreds.

// ref: instant DOM write, zero re-renders during drag
const viewBoxRef = useRef({ x: 0, y: 0, w: 1000, h: 700 });
// state: triggers React re-render — only on mouseup
const [viewBox, setViewBox] = useState(viewBoxRef.current);

onMouseMove = (e) => {
    viewBoxRef.current = newViewBox(e);
    svgEl.setAttribute("viewBox", toStr(viewBoxRef.current)); // instant
};
onMouseUp = () => setViewBox(viewBoxRef.current); // single re-render on drop
Decoupling imperative DOM updates from React's render cycle is a general pattern — useful any time you have high-frequency events (mouse, scroll, animation frames) that shouldn't trigger full re-renders.
04
Framework 4 — Graph Visualization · Disappointment

Sigma.js doesn't support deterministic layouts

ForceAtlas2 is a physics simulation — it runs until it "settles," and the result is different every time. For an exploratory network this is fine. For a tool where users need to build a mental map of their codebase, the graph shifting every reload is a bad experience.

  • Sigma's API is built entirely around its own lifecycle (start, stop, refresh) — plugging in a custom layout means fighting the framework, not using it
  • Graphology (the underlying data structure) is solid and well-documented — the problem is specifically Sigma's renderer layer
  • Alternative: d3-force with a fixed random seed gives reproducible results — but adds another dependency for one feature
  • We ended up with SVG + manual layout in ~200 lines — less than the time it would have taken to fight Sigma
A graph library optimized for exploratory visualization is the wrong tool when you need a stable, structured layout. Know what your visualization is for before picking the framework.
Takeaways

What We'd Tell Our Past Selves

  • LangGraph: astream_events is the right primitive for live streaming — but there's no graceful stop; breaking causes noise you can't silence cleanly
  • GitNexus: any npm package built on a native C++ library will silently fail on a fresh Docker image — always check for native dependencies early; also ~$1/PR is expensive at scale
  • FastAPI SSE: POST + SSE is a great combo; just know EventSource won't help you — roll your own fetch reader
  • Graph viz: reach for the framework last — if your layout is specific and deterministic, 200 lines of custom SVG beats 3 days fighting a general-purpose library
  • General: version numbers matter — langchain-mcp-adapters 0.1.0 silently breaks patterns that work in every blog post written about it
Qlankr — Sprint 1
